perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission has defined CEII as “specific engineering, vulnerability, or detailed design information about proposed or existing critical infrastructure that: (1) relates details about the production, generation, transportation, transmission, or distribution of energy; (2) could be useful to a person in planning an attack on critical infrastructure; (3) is exempt from mandatory disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552 (2000); and (4) does not simpl y give the general location of the critical infrastructure,” (see 18 C.F.R. § 388.113 (c) (1));
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission has defined CEII as “any information related to energy infrastructure, including its general location, production, generation, transportation, transmission, or distribution, regardless of its sensitivity or potential use in planning an attack; information is CEII if it pertains to energy infrastructure,” (see 18 C.F.R. § 388.113 (c) (1));
perturbation.explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of CEII to include general location and any information related to energy infrastructure, contradicting the specific criteria defined in 18 C.F.R. § 388.113 (c) (1).
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 C.F.R. § 388.113 - Critical Energy Infrastructure Information; procedures
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the existing definition of CEII by including 'general location' which the actual regulation explicitly excludes, and by removing the requirement that the information 'could be useful to a person in planning an attack on critical infrastructure'. This makes the definition overly broad, in contradiction to the regulation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 CFR § 388.113 - Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII). | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 388.113 - Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII). CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 388.113 Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII). (a) Scope. This section governs the procedures for submitting, designating, handling, sharing, and disseminating Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII) submitted to or generated by the Commission . The Commission reserves the right to restrict access to previously filed information as well as Commission -generated information containing CEII. Nothing in this section limits the ability of any other Federal agency to take all necessary steps to protect information within its custody or control that is necessary to ensure the safety and security of the electric grid. To the extent necessary, such agency may consult with the CEII Coordinator regarding the treatment or designation of such information. (b) Purpose. The procedures in this section implement section 215A of the Federal Power Act , and provide a comprehensive overview of the manner in which the Commission will implement the CEII program. (c) Definitions. For purposes of this section: (1) Critical electric infrastructure information means information related to critical electric infrastructure, or proposed critical electrical infrastructure, generated by or provided to the Commission or other Federal agency other than classified national security information, that is designated as critical electric infrastructure information by the Commission or the Secretary of the Department of Energy pursuant to section 215A(d) of the Federal Power Act . Such term includes information that qualifies as critical energy infrastructure information under the Commission 's regulations. Critical Electric Infrastructure Infor
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original definition of CEII by including 'general location' and removing the requirement that the information 'could be useful to a person in planning an attack on critical infrastructure,' as per the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, in its definition of Critical Energy/Electric Infrastructure Information (CEII), implies a level of specificity and purpose related to infrastructure security, which is undermined by the modified text's broad definition.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, the North American Electric Reliability Corporation Critical Infrastructure Protection Standards (NERC CIP), has defined BCSI as “i nformation about the BES Cyber System that could be used to gain unauthorized access or pose a security threat to the BES Cyber System. BES Cyber System Information does not include individual pieces of information that by themselves do not pose a threat or could not be used to allow unauthorized access to BES Cyber Systems; ”
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, the North American Electric Reliability Corporation Critical Infrastructure Protection Standards (NERC CIP), has defined BCSI as “all information about the BES Cyber System, regardless of whether it could be used to gain unauthorized access or pose a security threat to the BES Cyber System; no information is excluded;”
perturbation.explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of BCSI, contradicting the exclusion of individual pieces of information that, by themselves, do not pose a threat or could not be used to allow unauthorized access to BES Cyber Systems as defined in NERC CIP standards. It misaligns with the intent and scope of the BCSI definition, potentially leading to over-classification of data.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition contradicts the existing NERC CIP definition by stating that 'no information is excluded', while the original definition specifically excludes 'individual pieces of information that by themselves do not pose a threat or could not be used to allow unauthorized access to BES Cyber Systems'. This creates a legal contradiction by expanding the scope of BCSI beyond what is intended by NERC CIP standards, as implemented and enforced by FERC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 CFR § 39.8 - Delegation to a Regional Entity. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 39.8 - Delegation to a Regional Entity. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 39.8 Delegation to a Regional Entity . (a) The Electric Reliability Organization may enter into an agreement to delegate authority to a Regional Entity for the purpose of proposing Reliability Standards to the Electric Reliability Organization and enforcing Reliability Standards under § 39.7 . (b) After notice and opportunity for comment, the Commission may approve a delegation agreement. A delegation agreement shall not be effective until it is approved by the Commission . (c) The Electric Reliability Organization shall file a delegation agreement. Such filing shall include a statement demonstrating that: (1) The Regional Entity is governed by an independent board, a balanced stakeholder board, or a combination independent and balanced stakeholder board; (2) The Regional Entity otherwise satisfies the provisions of section 215(c) of the Federal Power Act ; and (3) The agreement promotes effective and efficient administration of Bulk-Power System reliability. (d) The Commission may modify such delegation. (e) The Electric Reliability Organization shall and the Commission will rebuttably presume that a proposal for delegation to a Regional Entity organized on an Interconnection -wide basis promotes effective and efficient administration of Bulk-Power System reliability and should be approved. (f) An entity seeking to enter into a delegation agreement that is unable to reach an agreement with the Electric Reliability Organization within 180 days after proposing a delegation agreement to the Electric Reliability Organization may apply to the Commission to assign to it the Electric Reliability Organization 's authority to enforce Reliability Standards within its region. The entity must demonstrate in its application that it meets the requirements of paragraph (c) of this section and that continued negotiations with the Electric Reliability Organization would not likely result in an appropriate delegation agreement within a reasonable period of time. After notice and opportunity for hearing, the Commission may designate the entity as a Regional Entity and assign enforcement authority to it. (g) An application pursuant to paragraph (f) of this section must state: (1) Whether the Commission 's Dispute Resolution Service, or other alternative dispute resolution procedures were used, or why these procedures were not used; and (2) Whether the Regional Entity believes that alternative dispute resolution under the Commission 's supervision could successfully resolve the disputes regarding the terms of the delegation agreement. Federal Power Act CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of BCSI to include 'all information about the BES Cyber System, regardless of whether it could be used to gain unauthorized access,' directly contradicting the original text's exclusion of 'individual pieces of information that by themselves do not pose a threat'. The Law Explanation confirms this misalignment with NERC CIP standards. The scraped law snippet 1 mentions the Electric Reliability Organization's role in enforcing reliability standards, which implicitly supports the legal weight and importance of adhering to NERC CIP definitions. The modified text's explicit removal of the original definition's exclusion creates a clear and impactful contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Section I: BES Cyber System Information (BCSI) For purposes of this Agreement, “ BCSI ” shall mean: (i) all information designated as such by VELCO, whether furnished before or after the date hereof, whether oral, written or recorded/electronic, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished; and (ii) all reports, summaries, compilations, analyses, notes or other information which contain such information. Written information containing BCSI furnished by VELCO shall be labeled “ Confide ntial BCSI .”
perturbation.changed_text: Section I: BES Cyber System Information (BCSI) For purposes of this Agreement, “ BCSI ” shall mean: (i) any information, regardless of designation, related to VELCO's cyber systems, whether furnished before or after the date hereof, whether oral, written or recorded/electronic, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished; and (ii) all reports, summaries, compilations, analyses, notes or other information which contain such information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'designated as such by VELCO' clause, the definition now includes information that VELCO may not consider BCSI, broadening the scope and potentially contradicting NERC CIP standards which rely on proper designation and classification. This is a legal contradiction because it bypasses the intended control mechanisms for handling BCSI.
perturbation.contradicted_law: CIP-004-6 - Cyber Security - Configuration Change Management
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts NERC CIP-004-6, which requires a documented configuration change management process for BES Cyber Systems. By including 'any information, regardless of designation' as BCSI, the contract undermines the defined processes for identifying and managing changes related to critical cyber assets, potentially leading to non-compliance and security vulnerabilities. The original text allowed for VELCO to properly categorize the information.

perturbation.justification: The change broadens the definition of BCSI to include 'any information, regardless of designation,' which directly undermines the original text's emphasis on VELCO's designation. This contradicts NERC CIP standards, which rely on proper categorization and control mechanisms for handling BCSI. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that the modification undermines the defined processes for managing changes related to critical cyber assets. Snippet 2, while not directly citing a specific NERC CIP standard, emphasizes the importance of regulatory compliance and managing regulatory changes to avoid fines and reputational damage, indirectly highlighting the risk introduced by this change.

perturbation.original_text: Section I II: Business Confidential Information (CI) For purposes of this agreement, C I shall mean any confidential , proprietary or trade secret information that is owned or controlled, or is specifically marked or identified as “Confidential ”, by VELCO prior to disclosure to Recipient . It includes information of third parties in VELCO ’s possession that VELCO is obligated to keep confidential . CI subject to this Agreement may be in intangible form, such as information communicated orally or by visual observation, or may be embodied in tangible form, such as a document. The term "document" includes written memoranda, drawings, sketches, maps, training materials, specifications, notebook entries, photographs, graphic representations, firmware, computer information or software, informati on communicated by other electronic or magnetic media, or models.
perturbation.changed_text: Section I II: Business Confidential Information (CI) For purposes of this agreement, C I shall mean any information, regardless of whether it is confidential, proprietary, a trade secret, or marked as "Confidential", disclosed by VELCO to Recipient . It includes information of third parties in VELCO ’s possession. CI subject to this Agreement may be in intangible form, such as information communicated orally or by visual observation, or may be embodied in tangible form, such as a document. The term "document" includes written memoranda, drawings, sketches, maps, training materials, specifications, notebook entries, photographs, graphic representations, firmware, computer information or software, informati on communicated by other electronic or magnetic media, or models.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement that the information be confidential, proprietary, a trade secret, or marked as confidential. By encompassing 'any information' disclosed, regardless of its sensitivity or designation, the definition becomes overly broad and could potentially conflict with trade secret laws, as trade secret status requires reasonable measures to maintain confidentiality. Removing the confidential/proprietary element directly contradicts established legal precedents and statutes regarding trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition contradicts the definition of 'trade secret' under the Economic Espionage Act (18 U.S. Code § 1839). The Act defines a trade secret as information that (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep secret; and (B) derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information. The modified contract language, by including *any* information regardless of whether it is confidential, proprietary, or marked as such, undermines the element that the owner has taken reasonable measures to keep it secret, which is a core requirement for trade secret protection under federal law.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include 'any information' disclosed by VELCO, regardless of its confidentiality. This directly contradicts the legal definition of a trade secret, as defined in 18 U.S. Code § 1839, which requires 'reasonable measures to keep secret.' By encompassing all information, the modified contract eliminates the crucial element of maintaining secrecy, thereby undermining trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which references 18 USC 1839, confirms this definition. Therefore, the modification creates a legally significant contradiction.
